policy_statement: Remodel urban stormwater drainage networks to hydraulically adjust to heavy rainfall flows, particularly in critical bottlenecks (bridges, aqueducts, etc.).
mechanism_description: Upgrading stormwater drainage networks increases hydraulic capacity during extreme rainfall, reducing flooding impacts at critical bottlenecks.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (extreme rainfall) + built environment upgrade → ↓ floodwater depth/extent/duration → ↓ exposure & damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in system: Cities redesign/upsize/retrofit stormwater pipes, culverts, inlets, channels, and bottleneck structures (e.g., at bridges/aqueducts) to handle higher peak flows.  
2) Mediating physical process: Increased hydraulic conveyance and reduced backwater/flow restriction lowers surcharge and surface ponding; faster drainage reduces flood duration at critical nodes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced frequency/severity of pluvial (surface water) flooding and associated impacts on people, property, and critical infrastructure under more intense precipitation extremes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing urban flood impacts (exposure and damage) during heavier rainfall events by increasing stormwater conveyance capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Extreme rainfall hazard is managed through engineered drainage capacity increases, leading to reduced flooding impacts—this is risk reduction.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The direct intended and largest effect is lowering flood exposure/damage, not reducing GHG emissions or energy/material use; any emissions effects from construction/operations are incidental and not the mechanism’s core climate benefit.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—mitigation/resource-efficiency/nature-based outcomes are not unavoidable consequences of drainage upgrades (they depend on design choices like green infrastructure, energy-efficient pumps, or materials).  
4) Edge cases applied: If upgrades are explicitly “green stormwater infrastructure” (bioswales, permeable surfaces) the mechanism could shift toward Nature-Based Solutions; if the system is primarily pumped with major energy implications, mitigation could be a context-dependent co-effect but not inherent.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy using “stormwater drainage network upgrades to handle heavy rainfall” consistently functions as adaptation by reducing pluvial flood risk.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “upgrade” is implemented via infiltration/green infrastructure (not just gray conveyance) or includes large pumped systems with significant operational energy changes, additional categories could apply, but those are not inherent to the canonical mechanism as stated.
additional_secondary_evidence: Flooding: Portugal faces flooding, and remodelling/retrofitting stormwater drainage networks at bottlenecks directly reduces exposure/sensitivity because increased hydraulic conveyance during heavy rainfall lowers surface ponding and flood depth/extent/duration in urban areas.
key_indicators: “Remodelling of urban stormwater drainage networks”; “hydraulically adjust to flows in heavy rainfall events”; “critical areas such as bridges, aqueducts and other bottlenecks”
co_benefits: Reduced disruption to transport/critical infrastructure services; improved water quality via reduced combined sewer/overflow events (if applicable); avoided economic losses from urban inundation
instance_edge_case_notes: “Remodelling” could also occur via updated design standards/requirements, but the text most strongly indicates physical retrofit works (capital upgrades) rather than a regulatory/performance standard.
